title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Six: In which Howl expresses his feelings with green slime (Fragment 3)
text:
“ His feet itch!” grumbled Calcifer. There was a fog down in Porthaven. Calcifer was low among his logs, moving uneasily this way and that to avoid drips in the chimney. “How does he think I feel, stuck in a damp grate like this?”

“Then you’ll have to give me a hint at least about how to break your contract,” Sophie said, shaking out the gray-and-scarlet suit. “Goodness, you’re a fine suit, even if you are a bit worn! Built to pull in the girls, aren’t you?”

“I have given you a hint!” Calcifer fizzed.

“Then you’ll have to give it me again. I didn’t catch it,” Sophie said as she laid the suit down and hobbled to the door.

“If I give you a hint and tell you it’s a hint, it will be information, and I’m not allowed to give that,” Calcifer said. “Where are you going?”

“To do something I didn’t dare do until they were both out,” Sophie said. She twisted the square knob over the door until the black blob pointed downward. Then she opened the door.

There was nothing outside. It was neither black, nor gray, nor white. It was not thick, or transparent. It did not move. It had no smell and no feel. When Sophie put a very cautious finger out into it, it was neither hot nor cold. It felt of nothing. It seemed utterly and completely nothing.

“What is this?” she asked Calcifer.

Calcifer was as interested as Sophie. His blue face was leaning right out of the grate to see the door. He had forgotten the fog. “I don’t know,” he whispered. “I only maintain it. All I know is that it’s on the side of the castle that no one can walk around. It feels quite far away.”

“It feels beyond the moon!” said Sophie. She shut the door and turned the knob green-downward. She hesitated a minute and then started to hobble to the stairs.

“He’s locked it,” said Calcifer. “He told me to tell you if you tried to snoop again.”

“Oh,” said Sophie. “What has he got up there?”

“I’ve no idea,” said Calcifer. “I don’t know anything about upstairs. If you only knew how frustrating it is! I can’t even really see outside the castle. Only enough to see what direction I’m going in.”

Sophie, feeling equally frustrated, sat down and began mending the gray-and-scarlet suit. Michael came in quite soon after that.

“The King saw me at once,” he said. “He—” He looked round the room. His eyes went to the empty corner where the guitar usually stood. “Oh, no!” he said. “Not the lady friend again! I thought she’d fallen in love with him and it was all over days ago. What’s keeping her?”

Calcifer fizzed wickedly. “You got the signs wrong. Heartless Howl is finding this lady rather tough. He decided to leave her alone a few days to see if that would help. That’s all.”

“Bother!” said Michael. “That’s bound to mean trouble. And here was I hoping Howl was almost sensible again!”

Sophie banged the suit down on her knees. “Really!” she said. “How can you both talk like that about such utter wickedness! At least, I suppose I can’t blame Calcifer, since he’s an evil demon. But you, Michael—!”

“I don’t think I’m evil,” Calcifer protested.

“But I’m not calm about it, if that’s what you think!” Michael said. “If you knew the trouble we’ve had because Howl will keep falling in love like this! We’ve had lawsuits, and suitors with swords, and mothers with rolling pins, and fathers and uncles with cudgels. And aunts. Aunts are terrible. They go for you with hat pins. But the worst is when the girl herself finds out where Howl lives and turns up at the door, crying and miserable. Howl goes out through the back door and Calcifer and I have to deal with them all.”

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Six: In which Howl expresses his feelings with green slime

vocabulary.definition: a secondary or less visible entrance
vocabulary.example: Howl goes out through the back door and Calcifer and I have to deal with them all.

vocabulary.definition: certain or destined to happen
vocabulary.example: That’s bound to mean trouble.

vocabulary.definition: showing care to avoid potential problems or dangers
vocabulary.example: When Sophie put a very cautious finger out into it, it was neither hot nor cold.

vocabulary.definition: a formal agreement between two or more parties
vocabulary.example: Then you’ll have to give me a hint at least about how to break your contract.

vocabulary.definition: short thick sticks used as weapons
vocabulary.example: and fathers and uncles with cudgels.

vocabulary.definition: slightly wet, often in an unpleasant way
vocabulary.example: How does he think I feel, stuck in a damp grate like this?

vocabulary.definition: to take action to solve a problem or handle a situation
vocabulary.example: Calcifer and I have to deal with them all.

vocabulary.definition: made a bubbling or hissing sound
vocabulary.example: I have given you a hint! Calcifer fizzed.

vocabulary.definition: feeling annoyed or less confident because you cannot achieve what you want
vocabulary.example: Sophie, feeling equally frustrated, sat down.

vocabulary.definition: causing annoyance or upset because of inability to achieve something
vocabulary.example: If you only knew how frustrating it is! I can’t even really see outside the castle.

vocabulary.definition: complained in a bad-tempered way
vocabulary.example: His feet itch! grumbled Calcifer.

vocabulary.definition: long pins for securing hats, sometimes used as weapons
vocabulary.example: Aunts are terrible. They go for you with hat pins.

vocabulary.definition: showing no kindness or consideration
vocabulary.example: Heartless Howl is finding this lady rather tough.

vocabulary.definition: paused before saying or doing something, especially due to uncertainty
vocabulary.example: She hesitated a minute and then started to hobble to the stairs.

vocabulary.definition: walked with difficulty, typically because of pain from an injury
vocabulary.example: Sophie said as she laid the suit down and hobbled to the door.

vocabulary.definition: legal actions brought to settle disputes in court
vocabulary.example: We’ve had lawsuits, and suitors with swords.

vocabulary.definition: to keep something in good condition or operation
vocabulary.example: I only maintain it. All I know is that it’s on the side of the castle that no one can walk around.

vocabulary.definition: repairing something that is broken or torn
vocabulary.example: Sophie sat down and began mending the gray-and-scarlet suit.

vocabulary.definition: expressed an objection or disagreement
vocabulary.example: I don’t think I’m evil, Calcifer protested.

vocabulary.definition: to secretly try to find out private things about someone
vocabulary.example: He told me to tell you if you tried to snoop again.

vocabulary.definition: men who court or seek to marry a woman
vocabulary.example: We’ve had lawsuits, and suitors with swords, and mothers with rolling pins.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows discomfort or anxiety
vocabulary.example: Calcifer was low among his logs, moving uneasily this way and that to avoid drips in the chimney.

vocabulary.definition: complete and extreme evil or immorality
vocabulary.example: How can you both talk like that about such utter wickedness!

vocabulary.definition: completely or absolutely
vocabulary.example: It seemed utterly and completely nothing.

vocabulary.definition: in a cruel or evil manner; playfully mischievous
vocabulary.example: Calcifer fizzed wickedly.
